content.full_text:
July 29, 1975


Mr. A. L. Libra, General Counsel
California Manufacturers Association
Post Office Box 1138
Sacramento, California 95805

Dear Mr. Libra:

                    Re: Request for Opinion
                        No. 75-153

Thank you for your letter dated July 7, 1975, enclosing a
copy of your opinion request which was dated April 14, 1975, but
which was not received by the Commission.

Your request for a formal opinion is denied because it raises
no substantial question involving interpretation of the Political
Reform Act.

The Commission is considering proposed regulations which will
answer your question. While these have not yet been adopted, they
are on the agenda for final consideration on August 6-7. A copy
of proposed regulation 18620 is enclosed.

The pertinent language is found in the proposed paragraphs
(a)(2)(c) and (a)(4) defining what is to be reported and in para-
graph (b) showing how such reportable items are to be disclosed.

Specifically, these regulations, if adopted, would not require
that you list the name of or amount paid to any employee if less
than $1,000 in gross compensation is attributable to his or her
lobbying effort. If more than that is reasonably allocated, the
name, title and amount must be disclosed.

We would suggest that you stay in touch to determine when
and in what form these proposed regulations will be finally adopted
by the Commission.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman, for the
                                        Commission

DHL:cjb
Enclosure

Request No. [illegible]
Date Received 4/10/75
Response Due 4/24/75

April 14, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
926 J Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

This is a request for an opinion upon a matter of common concern to a fair number of our members who have headquarters in other states. They find it necessary to send corporate employees into California frequently, to appear before legislative committees or state agencies, to furnish information and to testify upon various matters, which generally come within the definition in the act of "influencing legislative or administrative action".

Under Section 86109-c, the employer is required to report such payments for this purpose, including the name and address of the recipient, and a description of the consideration received. The method of reporting has been prescribed, by use of Form 650, with such information to be entered in Part 2 on page 2.

Section 82045-d is the definition of such payments, and provides for including "compensation for services" in connection with such activities. There is nothing in either the act or in regulations adopted to date, so far as we know, which specifies any further details of such reporting, such as allocation by the amount of time devoted during a visit to the state, for reportable activities and for other activities not within the purview of the act.

Neither do we find anything which specifies details of reporting such payments when more than one corporate employee travel to California to appear at the same meeting or hearing. Some of our members have corporate policies on disclosure of employee compensation, which provides for confidential treatment to the fullest extent possible, while complying with all applicable laws.

The question now being submitted for an opinion is the following:

"If two or more employees of the same corporate employer, who qualifies as a lobbyist employer under the act, travel to California together for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, is it permissible to report on Form 650 the compensation paid such employees in a lump sum, without specifying the amount payable to each individual?"

-2-

A[?] 14, 1975

The employer will of course have complete and detailed records of such
payments, which will be available to the Franchise Tax Board in performing its
audit duties.

Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions regarding this.


Sincerely yours,


A. L. LIBRA, General Counsel

ALL/dw
